Title: Default Value for Optional Node Inputs
Description: New nodes default to having Use Default Value turned on for all optional inputs. However, when packages are updated and existing nodes get additional optional inputs, graphs with these existing nodes do not update with Use Default Value turned on. This causes those nodes not to run and graphs to fail. Existing instances of nodes should also default to Use Default Value when package updates introduce additional optional inputs. Nodes get updated without turning on Use Default Value and end up not executing.

Title: Geometry modification operations do not result in uniquely identifiable topology!
Description: Every time you modify a solid in Dynamo, its edges and faces that get split, inherit the hash code of the original input geometry. New hash code for each element, uniquely different from the hashes of all other hash codes of the specific geometric entity instance. the hashes are the same as the input's:    This is definitely a pretty limited scenario but it's going to a big headache any time we want to modify something in dynamo and later try to rebuild its topological hierarchy. We can't map a face's co-edges to the actual bounding edges, because the hashes for edges that got split up will be doubled or trippled up. So when we try to export a correct Brep representational data from Dynamo to another medium, we'll keep fetching the wrong edges and our edge loops will fail to bind the faces.

Title: Graph is re-evaluated before UI interaction is complete
Description: It seems that disconnecting a port from a node triggers the graph execution when in automatic mode, even if the UI interaction hasn't finished (click on canvas to completed disconnect or connect to another port).  1. Start debugging the Refinery Toolkits (RT) from VS 2. Open known good graph in Automatic mode - runs fine 3. Disconnect one of the inputs into an RT node (an optional parameter in C method) 4. ArgumentNullException is caught in Visual Studio 5. Click continue in debugger 6. Finish UI interaction by clicking somewhere on canvas to complete port unplug operation I did not expect the graph to be re-evaluated when a UI interaction such as node disconnection or unplug/replug is not yet concluded. Surely it's un-necessary as the graph will never be in a desired state until that interaction is complete, whether it's unplugging or replugging a port.  Re-executing the graph during the UI interaction could negatively impact the responsiveness of the UI as they're not always on separate threads from what i understand (Dynamo Revit). ArgumentNullException is caught in Visual Studio as soon as port is disconnected, meaning execution of that node was just attempted.

Title: Geometry Working Range indiscriminately limits floating point precision causing failures where decimal places are required beyond the limit
Description: Simple XYZ coincident test between two points, rounded to 4DP (typical approach I take with such tests regardless of which computational design tool I use to avoid floating point inaccuracies). The requirement was to use the result to establish if sets of lines overlap and remove them. For the coincidence test to return true in every valid case within the rounded precision input. With precision rounded, I expect every coincident result to return true. However, the test returned false in places even though the points were unquestionably coincident. I found it was caused by the Geometry Working Range. The size of the model in question is not large: 260m x 260m.  As Geometry Working Range limits the DP, any calculations are limited to the same number of DPs. Where DPs matter, like for example, when checking if two points are coincident, this feature becomes a hindrance. The major problem is this: without increasing the Geometry Working Range, Dynamo wont output the geometry. When its increased to output the geometry, the DP limitation then scuppers any workflows which depend on a predefined number of DPs.   One solution is to make the predefined precision bigger (i.e. less DP = less accuracy = not good) and therein lies the problem. Any node that hard-codes precision will fail/function inconsistently as a result of the geometry working range (I have published a node called Curves.RemoveDuplicates with a hard-coded precision of 6DP - it fails to return accurate results as a consequence of the Geometry Working Range, see examples below).   To compound the problem, any node which has a precision input (like OOTB Point.PruneDuplicates) will also work inconsistently, and any graphs which worked fine prior to the introduction of the Geometry Working Range feature that include such nodes, may inadvertently break once the user upgrades to newer versions which do include it...and then they have to work out its this phenomenon causing the problem!   Hence, I regard this a quite a serious issue as it makes the use of Dynamo inconsistent and unpredictable or adds an unnecessary complication to any geometry-based node.   cc @kronz this is the 'major' issue with Geometry Working Range I mentioned on Twitter.  FYI @radumg  Examples: The following example worked without issue prior to the introduction of Geometry Working Range.  DWG import of a road. The intention is to convert the element into a floor. The requirement is to first, exatract the top faces, then get their edges and test for coincidence by testing where centre points are coincident. By virtue of the faces being derived from a valid solid entity, the only overlapping curves will be guaranteed where two of its faces touch and the outer edges never overlap, making them easy to identify. In addition, the centre points are guaranteed to be consistent as the edges are lines.  The curves to test    The result after processing  Fails when with Geometry Working Range is set to medium (which is 4DP according to the dialog box in Dynamo) Why? Because the script is designed to only return perimeter curves if no coincident centre points are found, and we know any curve that isn't a perimeter curve will have an overlap. Hence, where 'webbing' is occurring is where the DP rounding cap prevents the correct result from being yield (The script tests XYZ coordinates and uses the Python round() method with a predefined DP of 6 internally to ascertain coincidence):   Increasing the DP within the script If I re-run the script this time increasing the predefined DP to 4, works as expected. The problem I have with this is in situations where tolerance is mission critical. In these cases, Dynamo cant be used due to this problem, not to mention why it isn't documented anywhere and why it even occurs in the first place:    Curves.RemoveDuplicates In another example, Curves.RemoveDuplicates uses an internal DP precision of 6 to basically capture practically any duplicate. But since this is higher than the DP cap imposed by the Geometry Working Range, it outputs inconsistent results or fails to work (it should have found 208 duplicates, but instead finds none - and no, its not a problem with the node itself!):    I'm used to working without restrictions on DP and, while maybe being pedantic, I would expect this type of limitation to be clearly flagged up at the very least, especially for engineering scenarios where precision matters.

Title: Dynamo 2.0 nodes cannot unpack dictionaries!
Description: Tried to work on a list of dictionaries a result The core nodes provided no error, just null value for each dict. The core D4R nodes actually provided a useful error: >Warning: Element.Name expects argument type(s) (Revit.Elements.Element), but was called with (DesignScript.Builtin.Dictionary).    I would have expected that we would automatically get the values of the dictionary items as an unsorted list and that the design script VM would map our function to each value item in that unsorted list. Instead we get the above. If we add a Dictionary.Values node, we can fix the result but that seems counter-intuitive to the way things just worked before.

Title: Curve.PointAtEqualChordLength
Description: On a 2 identic Lines I used the 2 functions Curve.PointsAtEqualSegmentLength and CurveAt EqualChordLength with the same number of divisions (10). As the line extremity points are not included in this list this function is not dividing the line into 10 parts as requested but 11. In order to obtain the same result on the same line with those 2 function I had to define a number of divisions equal to 10 for the function Curve.PointsAtEqualSegmentLength (see Line 3 group in the attached graph) and a number of divisions equal to 9 for the function CurveAt EqualChordLength (see Line 4 group in the attached graph). Title: Minor - RunCompleted event is never invoked. (Seems deprecated).
Description: Hit run (also happens when set to automatic). The event RunComplete in DynamoModel should be invoked once the graph has finish execution. The event is not fired. Looking through the source this event is just never invoked. It looks as though this event is deprecated (or was never used). Instead the event EvaluationComplete is invoked on graph completion instead. The event RunComplete and method OnRunComplete are public facing members so should be deprecated. Users should be notified that EvalutionComplete is the correct event to subscribe to.

Title: Package manager (webhook) integrations
Description: Wanted to update a package I had already published. - ability to use DynamoSandbox to publish package/update and not have to use Revit - ability specify which files were updated or some instructions Dynamo for Revit allows you to re-publish the folder where it downloaded & installed the package as a new version.  You thus have to : - install package from package manager - overwrite DLL/dyf files where Revit installed them - publish new version from Revit  This process is never explained in the UI and there's no way to change this behaviour. Had to publish an update to the update to get it right 👎 I would like to be able to update a package simply by : - publishing a new release on GitHub for example - pushing to a designated branch on git  Ideally, we'd have a webhook integration, which would support both scenarios above and then some.  If you're planning to decouple the package manager from this repo, please take this issue with it.

Title: On Workspace changed, new instance of AstBuilder and Profiling reseted
Description: Simple example on enabling profiling on Workspace changed.  csharp private void OnWorkspaceChanged(IWorkspaceModel workspace) {     HomeWorkspaceModel homeWorkspace = workspace as HomeWorkspaceModel;      if (homeWorkspace == null)         return;      homeWorkspace.EngineController.EnableProfiling(true, homeWorkspace, homeWorkspace.Nodes); } Profiling enabled for the newly opened workspace. Profiling can be enabled only when the current EngineController's private AstBuilder property doesn't have an active ProfilingSession. This is fine but for the fact that when opening a new file (changing the workspace), the EngineController gets reseted with a new instance and subsequently, its AstBuilder.  Problem is that this reset happens after the WorkspaceChanged event gets fired, so by the time we can enable profiling on the subscribed event, the EngineController gets reseted and profiling back to be disabled.    csharp private bool OpenJsonFileFromPath(string fileContents, string filePath, bool forceManualExecutionMode) {     try     {         DynamoPreferencesData dynamoPreferences = DynamoPreferencesDataFromJson(fileContents);         if (dynamoPreferences != null)         {             // TODO, QNTM-1101: Figure out JSON migration strategy             if (true) //MigrationManager.ProcessWorkspace(dynamoPreferences.Version, xmlDoc, IsTestMode, NodeFactory))             {                 WorkspaceModel ws;                 if (OpenJsonFile(filePath, fileContents, dynamoPreferences, forceManualExecutionMode, out ws))                 {                     OpenWorkspace(ws); // <-- THIS FIRES 'WorkspaceChanged' EVENT                      //Raise an event to deserialize the view parameters before                     //setting the graph to run                     OnComputeModelDeserialized();                      SetPeriodicEvaluation(ws); // <-- THIS RESETS 'EngineController' AND SUBSEQUENTLY DISABLES PROFILING                 }             }         }         return true;     }     catch (Exception e)     {         Console.WriteLine(e.Message);         return false;     } }   cc'ing @mjkkirschner @ColinDayOrg

Title: [Wish] Be able to install daily builds side by side with stable builds
Description: Be able to install stable and daily builds side by side The daily build would overwrite the current stable build in use  Right now every time I'd like to test a new bug fix I'll have to: - uninstall my current stable build - install the daily build - restore all my settings, custom nodes and packages - test whatever I need to test - uninstall the daily build - re-install the stable build - restore all my settings, custom nodes and packages - hope nothing goes wrong between installs and Windows registry doesn't !@$ the bed as usual  As much as we would like to always use the greatest and latest available, when we're doing work, we expect the best stability currently available and have to keep an environment as closely resembling that of target the user base. This means that using a daily build for production is out of the question.  Having to go through the above list every time you need to test something, means nobody who actually uses dynamo on a daily basis is going to bother with the daily builds. New bugs and regressions will go unreported and will have a higher chance of reaching the stable builds and a higher chance of angry people knocking on your door, than necessary :)   If instead we could install daily builds side by side with the stable one and easily switch between the two, the usage of dailies will definitely go up  I've heard of people using virtual desktops and similar to try and ease testing but this is something prohibitively difficult for the larger audience. If you have any *simple* alternative solutions to testing daily builds, I would love to hear them - even better let's make a wiki guide and link to spread awareness.

Title: Dynamo search less effective
Description: Search for nodes The nodes I was searching for. Unrelated nodes in many cases, or nothing at all.  After installing Revit 2020, the new dynamo presented issues when searching for specific nodes. The search results were too limited and I had trouble finding what I want by typing key words, which in some cases turned up nothing. Whereas in the previous version it was much easier to find what I wanted.  The only way to work around this currently would be to type the node name exactly as it is (with points and exact word order), which kind of defeats the purpose of having a search.  I came to understand that there were changes made to the dynamo search to make it faster, but this happened at the expense of the ability to quickly get to the required node.  I am including a link to the [thread on the dynamo forum](where this issue was discussed by multiple affected users.

Title: ZeroTouch Nodes - Node return type information missing for some zero-touch nodes.
Description: Created a zero-touch node that returns an object who's ToString method returns a string starting with a bracket, for example a Tuple. The node should indicate what type is returned, or something other than an empty string. The node information bubble contains an empty string.    I've traced the bug back to the method ProcessThing in CompactBubbleHandler.   Using the Tuple as an example, when a tuple passes through this function it will enter the else block and since a Tuple.ToString returns  (T1, T2) the above code will remove everything after the first bracket and return an empty string.  ---  I understand this will only happen for types that aren't defined internally within Dynamo. So maybe this method should return External Type or something along those lines instead of an empty string?

Title: Connectors lose connection after editing a custom or Formula node.
Description: 2019.2.0.65 Edit formula in a formula node. Same issue have been seen when editing other nodes as well. None other changes than the formula I edited. The Formula node seems to be losing it      s input (one I created, which worked fine before editing). It looks as if the Formula node is still connected to the node providing the input, e.g. a GetParameterValue node. If I delete the Formula node, the connector from the GetParameterValue node is hanging, not connected to anything.   Only solution (that I have found), is to copy/paste, in this case, both the formula node and the node providing the input, and then delete the faulty nodes.  I have seen this with other nodes as well, where several outputs, suddenly are connected to the same output.  Searching through existing threads handling the same topic, I found this from 2016.    Hopefully, I managed to describe a way to recreate the issue. I started a new thread, since both Dynamo and Revit versions are more recent than the ones used in found thread.

Title: Consistency of CTRL+Select vs SHIFT+Select in Revit vs. Dynamo
Description: Selected multiple nodes with CTRL+Select Select multiple objects, because that's how it works in Revit Deselects all nodes.  In Revit, (somewhat idiosyncratically) CTRL+Select adds objects to the selection, while SHIFT+Select deletes objects from the selection set.   In Dynamo, I frequently group nodes to organize my graphs, and it works the opposite-- SHIFT+Select adds items to the selection, while CTRL+Select appears to deselect everything.   I can almost wrap my head around the way selection works differently between Microsoft, Adobe, and Autodesk products, but I think when I switch between Revit and Dynamo, selection should work the same.    So if CTRL+Select adds things to the selection in Revit, that's how it should work in Dynamo too.

Title: Outside key or variable link
Description: Created a large, complex graph. An option to export Native Dynamo data/geometry to another Dynamo file. Flux plugin only.  I like flux, and I will gladly pay $60 a month for the plugin, but it seems like Autodesk and Dynamo should provide Dynamo to Dynamo link natively.  This greatly helps partition workflows, allow multiple users for larger workflows, reduce risk of file corruption and losing work due to crashes, inherantly provide milestones, and the top benefit is it speeds up graphs, especially on the first run.  Even if, as Ian K. suggests, more dynamo functionality is partitioned out into packages, the option for referencing/linking, perhaps via the 360 cloud, would be faster than an external plugin.

Title: Geometry Working Range makes code fail unless tolerance is set to unacceptable level.
Description: Tried to create a Surface patch with clean closed tangent arcs and lines with a geometry range set at Medium (0.0001 to 10,000). As need to calculate area and have not found a good node to do it with just closed curves that also works in refinery.  I know Dynamo is unit-less but this is the best tolerance i can find for working in Meters. A surface patch with out warnings And without having to change the working range up to Large which although works means my calculation tolerance is 10mm which is not acceptable in design. As there is no geometry scaling option in-between Medium and Large. A surface patch failing and an error :  Warning: Curve.Patch operation failed. Unable to make wire from edge body WIRE_SELF_INTERSECTS2 -- Wire has self-intersection.  This is not the first time i have had this issue with geometry working range in dynamo. See this post.   I also think its linked to this bug report that seems to yet to be solved I must say the idea that i have to keep changing the geometry scaling to get certain nodes to work is crazy!!. Grasshopper is also unit-less but has not issues with what ever you create, you don't have to keep changing the tolerance to get the code to work. It is even an issue with some node that they fail such as intersections, duplicate points, etc. Because the tolerance is set to high because another node fails otherwise. A lose lose situation.    Additionally changing the tolerances also change the output from Arc Radius components etc when creating an arc from intersecting circles where the circle radius matches what's in the slider and the  arc has some random set of numbers after the decimal point if not set to small. Both these radius should be bang on the same number as the slider not with loads of extra numbers after unless they are 000! This issue also effects writing data to excel and then reading it to rebuild the arcs in another file as unless you leave the long list of numbers at the end for every point, angle etc it fails to join the arcs in the next code.

Pull a category, filter for 4 different families in the category. One family reports, the others show null. At different attempts, with the full graph, I get two of the four. I used to see three of the four. It seems to be a declining situation. Dynamo to push Excel-originated lists into Revit family parameters. Failure without error messages, but VM failure message  [X JUST FAMILY CALL FROM REVIT.zip](

Title: Closing workspace does not clear extensions on right sidebar
Description: 1. Build Dynamo from source 2. Open graph & make sure right sidebar is open with DocumentationBrowser view extension loaded 3. Close graph Sidebar to disappear A specific control remains on screen instead of being hidden.  This is because closing the workspace simply hides the right sidebar instead of actually clearing the tabs. Behaviour when manually closing the sidebar is correct 👍      When closing the workspace without closing the sidebar, control is still visible 💥 Now, this might not be a Dynamo issue as the other extensions (like WorkspaceDependency...) behave correctly and it appears it's only the WebView control that misbehaves as the banner control I load correctly disappears. Could be that WebView does not inherit visibility properly.  However, wanted to raise it as others might encounter it with their custom controls. Is this the desired behaviour when closing the workspace? Or would we want to clear the tab items ?  Adding an event that does TabItems.Clear() when the workspace is closed solves the issue obvs.  FYIs @QilongTang  @mjkkirschner

Title: Node Input Schemes
Description: See below. See below. I didn't know where else to put this, so here it is:  Blanket statement:  All below suggestions assume performance speed is not greatly reduced by implementation.  Some suggestions could be implemented via an optional UI switch.  List@Level - Adjusts area of list focus PRIOR to entering nodes.  It’s awesome even if its dependency upon Top-down list hierarchy slows performance (compared to GH) a bit.  It cannot be expected to solve node behavior once data enters into the node.  It effectively replaces GH nodes like Shift list among others.  It effectively reduces dependency on DS nodes such as list.chop, list.flatten and some others whilst allowing the user to retain more of the list structure as needed, and with much fewer nodes.  Var, var[], and var[]..[]  -  These are useful for designing a custom node (or a built-in node).  Var looks only for a single item, var[] looks for a list.  But we should make it clear that var[]..[] is only used when upgrading a workflow that was initially built to handle a simple list—ie a LOFT Node built to create a SINGLE loft would be looking for 1 (maybe 2) inputs-- a single list of curves and perhaps 1 or 2 guide curves.  Assigning these expected formats to inputs should only be done in the workflow of converting a singular workflow to a workflow that handles a multiple of that condition.  A best practice, and maybe a useful restriction to this workflow, would be to “Only ever increase expected input list depth by one level when creating a Custom Node from a singular workflow”.  Here is a suggestion that may not be popular among automation enthusiasts, but with regard to Lacing and List.Combine, we may be looking for a shortcut (in the short term) that simply doesn’t exist.  The amount of use cases for geometry creation and data management is so vast that expecting to know them all up front may be unrealistic.  As Brian R. says: “Doing the same thing manually more than once IS Insanity”, BUT doing the same thing slightly differently more than once is WORK, and sometimes it can’t be avoided.  Read below to get the meaning:  During the growth of GH into a fluid tool—there was much back-and-forth about how to handle DEFAULT behavior for certain nodes (Dyn Team has/is undergoing similar discussions), especially simpler nodes such as line by points.  For example:  “What happens if input ‘A’ passes a list and ‘B’ passes a single object?!?”  This is the core of the frustration of a “Node with Inputs” type format.  Once the data enters the Node, IT’S IN.  Simpler nodes with fewer inputs can have a logical default behavior that most users would expect—but limiting, say, line creation to a specific expected input is LIMITING.  There are 2 obvious solutions:  1.	Create multiple nodes, one for each case--  This has happened to some extent already— ie. Geometry.Interesect and Geometry.IntersectALL etc.  2.	Create multiple possible schemes and embed them within a single node.  I agree with Andrew H.-- I create Custom Nodes to work with a single item workflow first, then wrap it in a Custom Node and pass multiples in.  I’ve created custom nodes with many inputs (>3) to work for a workflow specific to MY graph.  Most inputs expected a single variable—integer, double, string etc, and only 1 or 2 inputs expected a LIST.  This kept the node within the realm of solutions like Lacing and List.Combine.  But I have occasionally wanted to CONVERT additional inputs from expecting single items to expecting LISTS, but without losing my original configuration.  ENTER INPUT SCHEMES.  As additional use cases arise, additional custom node graphs can be created—using the same process as before, build for one workflow in the main canvas first and then wrap it inside the custom node to pass data with 1 level of increased depth. The difference is that MULTIPLE WORKFLOWS could be embedded in the SAME NODE.  This solution is similar to what Ant-B-Thorp was saying about a machine predicting what lacing a user wants (Machine learning could easily be inserted into this workflow later) but it is a little simpler and harnesses the inevitable growth in user contribution at all levels.  The Now Solution requires a mechanism to switch between schemes and an upgrade to the UI. UI - Inputs could have different color values (or lightness/darkness of grey for the color blind) to indicate if the active scheme expects a SINGLE VALUE (light orange?) or a LIST (deep orange).  The left inputs edge could be click-expanded to reveal input schemes for selection.  Alternatively, the user could click on a single input to change it from SINGLE to LIST or LIST to LISTofLISTS.  A little work here could automate scheme-change upon clicking a single input based on what/how many schemes are under the hood.  ADDITIONAL THOUGHTS Input Schemes are Scalable even if industrial for the majority of cases.  It helps eliminate many of the solutions (Lacing, List.Combine) which promised to be something much more but were really just solutions for specific cases.  Custom Nodes should have the same automated integration of List@Level as built-in nodes.  List@Level, if it doesn’t hamper future upgrades or greatly restrict performance/speed, should be committed to here.  It is critical to think about software as a closed system when it comes to speed.  Everything we add may slow performance down, so with every additive change it is important to ask “What can we remove”.  Cloud computing will make many things possible that were not possible when GH was being developed, and Dynamo should take advantage of this by allowing more sophistication/complexity into its engine by Embracing input schemes in native nodes as well as allowing users to build their own input schemes.  GH also had to get over the hump of making scripting cool—David R. and McNeel kept many of the decisions about node behavior locked down (while listening to forum suggestions).  But coding and its frame of mind are more mainstream now and will continue to grow.  Dynamo should anticipate this and continue to embrace whatever intermediate levels of fluency emerge and are required to propel sophistication forward.  The UI should not be ignored however.  Design is a very visual endeavor, and keeping both sides of the brain engaged throughout the process will yield better results.  Node inputs should also be visually distinct if they carry a default value.  I think this concept takes many users by surprise.  Replication guides, unless they are much faster than other methods, seem redundant to List@Level and INPUT SCHEMES.  My hunch is these could now go away or be buried a little more to not confuse newer users as they are DS only.  Also agree with (I can’t remember who) that Custom Nodes should be allowed to reside inside a Graph.dyn without being saved as an outside custom node.  Saving in same local folder is a good workaround, and creating packages has its benefits, but sometimes a one-off node really doesn’t want it to be separated from the mothership.  At first I thought this limitation was on purpose to promote code sharing, but the need is there.  Maybe this will go somewhere (or show us one way not to do it). Best Regards.
